If an aircraft or ship yaws, it moves slightly to the side of its intended direction
examples: The boat pitched and yawed in the bay.
Witnesses to the crash reported seeing the Challenger yaw to the right.

I felt a slight pitch and yaw, like a hiccup in the floor beneath me.

definitions.definition: a period of twelve months, especially from 1 January to 31 December
examples: Annette worked in Italy for two years.
2005 was one of the worst years of my life.
We went to Egypt on holiday last year.
At this time of year the beaches are almost deserted.
This species keeps its leaves all year (round) (= through the year).

definitions.definition: a period of twelve months relating to a particular activity
examples: The financial/tax year begins in April.

definitions.definition: the part of the year, in a school or university, during which courses are taught
examples: the academic/school year
She's now in her final/first/second year at Manchester University.
My daughter is in her freshman/sophomore/junior/senior year.

definitions.definition: a group of students who start school, college, university, or a course together
examples: Kathy was in the year above me at college.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a student in a particular year group at a school, college, or university

a first-year student

examples: She earns 400,000 yen a month as an English teacher in Tokyo.

examples: The yen fell/rose against (= was worth less/more compared to) the dollar today.

definitions.definition: a strong feeling of wanting or wishing for something
examples: I have a yen for travelling.

definitions.definition: in the past, a man who was not a servant and who owned and cultivated (= grew crops on) an area of land
in the past, a man who was not a servant and who owned and cultivated (= grew crops on) an area of land
yes
examples: "Do I press this button?" "Yep, that's right."

definitions.definition: used to express willingness or agreement
examples: "Would you like a glass of wine?" "Yes, thanks."
"Do you like Thai food?" "Yes, I love it."
"He's a really nice guy." "Yes, he is."
"Report to me at nine o'clock tomorrow morning." "Yes, sir."
"Have you had enough to eat?" "Yes, thank you."
If you'd say yes (= agree) to the request you'd avoid a lot of trouble.

definitions.definition: used for emphasis
examples: We visited all 50 states, yes sir, all 50.

definitions.definition: used to show that you are listening to someone, or that you are ready to listen and to give them an answer or information
examples: "Dad." "Yes, what do you want, honey?"
Yes, can I help you?

definitions.definition: used when you are disagreeing with a negative statement
examples: "I'm not a very good cook, though." "Yes, you are - you make wonderful food!"

definitions.definition: used at the end of a sentence to invite someone to agree with you or to check that you have understood something correctly
examples: It's better this way. Yes?
You are going to help me, yes?

definitions.definition: a vote supporting a particular plan of action or a reply accepting an invitation
examples: "Have you had any replies yet?" "Six yeses and two noes so far."

examples: "Have you had any replies yet?" "Six yeses and two noes so far."

definitions.definition: on the day before today
examples: He called yesterday while you were out.
I saw her yesterday afternoon.

definitions.definition: the day before today
examples: "Is that today's paper?" "No, it's yesterday's."

definitions.definition: two days ago
examples: I called her the day before yesterday.

definitions.definition: the recent past
examples: Nobody's interested in yesterday's pop stars.
These songs are a part of all our yesterdays.

definitions.definition: a time in the past
examples: the Hollywood stars of yesteryear

definitions.definition: still; until the present time
examples: I haven't spoken to her yet.
He hasn't finished yet.
"Are you ready?" "Not yet - wait a minute."

definitions.definition: the best, worst, etc. until now
examples: Of all the songs I've heard tonight, that's the best yet.

definitions.definition: until and including this time
examples: We haven't needed extra staff as yet, but we may in the future.
No ambulances had as yet managed to get across the river.

definitions.definition: from now and for a particular period of time in the future
examples: She won't be back for a long time yet.
Our holiday isn't for weeks yet.

definitions.definition: If you have yet to do something, you have not done it
examples: They have yet to make a decision.

definitions.definition: even at this stage or time
examples: We could yet succeed - you never know.
You might yet prove me wrong.
He may win yet.

definitions.definition: used to add emphasis to words such as another and again, especially to show an increase in amount or the number of times something happens
examples: Rachel bought yet another pair of shoes to add to her collection.
I'm sorry to bother you yet again.
He's given us yet more work to do.

definitions.definition: (and) despite that; used to add something that seems surprising because of what you have just said
examples: simple yet effective
He's overweight and bald, (and) yet somehow, he's attractive.

to supply or produce something positive such as a profit, an amount of food or information
examples: an attempt to yield increased profits
The investigation yielded some unexpected results.
Favourable weather yielded a good crop.

definitions.definition: to give up the control of or responsibility for something, often because you have been forced to
examples: They were forced to yield (up) their land to the occupying forces.
Despite renewed pressure to give up the occupied territory, they will not yield.

definitions.definition: to bend or break under pressure
examples: His legs began to yield under the sheer weight of his body.

definitions.definition: to stop in order to allow other vehicles to go past, especially before you drive onto a bigger road
examples: If you're going downhill, you need to yield to bikers going uphill.

definitions.definition: an amount of something positive, such as food or profit, that is produced or supplied
examples: Crop yields have risen steadily.
Yields on gas and electricity shares are consistently high.

definitions.definition: to stop in order to allow other vehicles to go past, especially before you drive onto a bigger road

definitions.definition: A yielding person can change the way they normally behave or deal with situations when it is helpful or necessary.

definitions.definition: If an animal such as a dog yips, it makes a short, sharp cry

definitions.definition: belonging or relating to the person or group of people being spoken or written to

definitions.definition: belonging or relating to people generally
examples: Of course you want the best for your children.

definitions.definition: said before a typical example of something is given
examples: This isn't your usual science fiction novel, but then Brinkworth isn't exactly your typical author.

definitions.definition: the one(s) belonging to or connected with the person or group of people being spoken or written to
examples: Is this pen yours?
Unfortunately my legs aren't as long as yours.

examples: Yours, Jack

definitions.definition: used to show that something belongs to or is connected with people generally
examples: Other people's children always seem to be better behaved than yours.

definitions.definition: used when the subject of the verb is "you" or the person being spoken to, and the object is the same person
examples: Be careful with that knife or you'll cut yourself!
Katie, control yourself!

definitions.definition: used for emphasis when the subject is "you"
examples: Did you make the dress yourself?
You can do that yourself.

definitions.definition: to behave in your usual manner, rather than behaving in a way you think other people might like
examples: The best thing you can do is to go into the interview and just be yourself.

definitions.definition: alone or without help from anyone else
examples: I'm amazed you managed to move those boxes all by yourself.
Do you like being by yourself sometimes or do you get lonely?

definitions.definition: for your use only
examples: So you have the whole house to yourself this weekend?

definitions.definition: not to be, seem, or feel as happy or healthy as usual
examples: Why don't you go home early if you're not feeling yourself?

definitions.definition: used when asking someone about their state of mind when they are physically ill
examples: I know you must still be uncomfortable, but how are you in yourself?

definitions.definition: used when both the subject and object of the verb are "you" and "you" is also being used to refer to people generally
examples: You tell yourself everything's all right but you know it's not really.

definitions.definition: plural of yourself

definitions.definition: the period of your life when you are young, or the state of being young
examples: I was a fairly good football player in my youth.

You may not have played tennis as often as him, but at least you've got youth on your side (= you are young).
